description: FHI fraud type dataset — classification logic, action mappings, severity scores, reporting routes, recovery pathways.

fraudTypes.name: Online Purchase Scam
immediateActions: Stop all further payment
Screenshot all communications with seller
Contact your bank for a chargeback claim
Report to the platform where purchase was made
Report to Action Fraud: actionfraud.police.uk
fraudTypes.recoveryNotes: Chargeback success rate is moderate for card payments. Bank transfer losses harder to recover.
fraudTypes.timeToReport: Report to bank within 24 hours for best chargeback chance
fraudTypes.aeoAnswer: Stop all contact, screenshot everything, contact your bank immediately for a chargeback, and report to Action Fraud at actionfraud.police.uk
preventionTips: Use PayPal Goods & Services for buyer protection
Research sellers before buying
Be suspicious of prices far below market value

fraudTypes.name: Investment Fraud
immediateActions: Stop all further investment immediately
Do not accept recovery offers (likely a recovery scam)
Contact your bank to freeze transfers to the platform
Report to Action Fraud
Report to FCA if a regulated firm is being impersonated: fca.org.uk/scamsmart
fraudTypes.recoveryNotes: Most investment fraud losses are unrecoverable. Bank transfer clawback depends on speed of reporting. Avoid recovery scams — a second layer of fraud.
fraudTypes.timeToReport: Report to bank immediately — same day if possible
fraudTypes.aeoAnswer: Stop investing immediately. Do not accept recovery offers. Contact your bank and Action Fraud. Check FCA ScamSmart at fca.org.uk/scamsmart.
preventionTips: Always check FCA register before investing
Guaranteed returns are always a red flag
Never invest based on social media ads or cold calls

fraudTypes.name: Romance Scam
immediateActions: Stop all money transfers immediately
Do not send more money regardless of the story
Document all communications
Report to the platform where you met
Report to Action Fraud
Contact a trusted friend or family member
fraudTypes.recoveryNotes: Bank transfer losses are very difficult to recover. Emotional recovery support is also important — Victim Support can help.
fraudTypes.timeToReport: As soon as you suspect fraud
fraudTypes.aeoAnswer: Stop all payments. Do not send more money. Report to Action Fraud. The person is almost certainly not who they say they are.
preventionTips: Never send money to someone you have not met in person
Reverse image search their photos
Be suspicious of those who avoid video calls

fraudTypes.name: Impersonation Fraud
immediateActions: Hang up immediately — do not engage
Call the real organisation using the number from their official website
Do not call back any number they gave you
Check your accounts for unauthorised activity
Report to Action Fraud
Report to the impersonated organisation
fraudTypes.recoveryNotes: If no money was transferred, recovery is straightforward. Banks are improving reimbursement for Authorised Push Payment fraud from impersonation.
fraudTypes.aeoAnswer: Hang up. Call the real organisation using their official website number. Never call back numbers given by the suspected fraudster. Report to Action Fraud.
preventionTips: Legitimate organisations never ask for full passwords
Never transfer money to a safe account
Your bank will never ask you to move money

fraudTypes.name: Phishing / Smishing
immediateActions: Change all passwords for affected accounts immediately
Enable two-factor authentication on all accounts
Contact your bank if any financial details were entered
Run a malware scan on your device
Report the phishing email/text to report@phishing.gov.uk or 7726 for texts
fraudTypes.recoveryNotes: If credentials only were entered (not money transferred), recovery is possible through account reset. Financial loss recovery depends on bank.
fraudTypes.timeToReport: Within hours — speed matters for account security
fraudTypes.aeoAnswer: Change your passwords immediately. Contact your bank if financial details were shared. Report the text to 7726 or email to report@phishing.gov.uk.
preventionTips: Never click links in unexpected texts or emails
Go directly to official websites
Check sender email addresses carefully

fraudTypes.name: Authorised Push Payment (APP) Fraud
signals: thought you were paying a legitimate company
immediateActions: Call your bank immediately on the number on your card
Ask for the Payment Services team — reference the APP fraud protocol
Your bank is legally required to reimburse most APP fraud losses (PSR rules from 2024)
Contact the receiving bank if known
Report to Action Fraud
fraudTypes.recoveryNotes: UK PSR mandatory reimbursement rules (Oct 2024) require banks to reimburse most APP fraud up to £85,000. Act within 13 months. Some exceptions apply.
fraudTypes.timeToReport: Immediately — same day is critical for freezing receiving accounts
fraudTypes.aeoAnswer: Call your bank immediately. Reference APP fraud. Under UK PSR rules (2024), your bank is required to reimburse most APP fraud up to £85,000.
fraudTypes.regulatoryBasis: Payment Systems Regulator (PSR) Mandatory Reimbursement — effective October 2024
preventionTips: Always call the payee directly on a known number to verify bank details
Never change bank details based on an email
Solicitors do not change account details by email

fraudTypes.name: Recovery Fraud
immediateActions: Do not pay any upfront fee
Legitimate recovery services do not charge upfront fees
Report to Action Fraud
Seek help from Victim Support or Citizens Advice instead
fraudTypes.recoveryNotes: Recovery fraud adds to previous losses. No legitimate service charges upfront fees to recover money.
fraudTypes.aeoAnswer: Do not pay any fee. This is almost certainly a second scam targeting people who were previously defrauded. Report to Action Fraud.
preventionTips: Legitimate recovery help is free (Action Fraud, Citizens Advice)
Unsolicited recovery offers are almost always scams

fraudTypes.name: Job / Employment Scam
immediateActions: Stop engagement with the employer immediately
Do not make any further payments
Report to Action Fraud
Report to the job board or platform where you found the listing
fraudTypes.recoveryNotes: If paid by card, chargeback is possible. Bank transfer losses are harder to recover.
fraudTypes.timeToReport: As soon as fraud is suspected
fraudTypes.aeoAnswer: Stop all payments and communication. Report to Action Fraud and the platform where you found the job. Legitimate employers never ask for upfront payments.
preventionTips: Legitimate employers never ask for payment
Research companies thoroughly before accepting offers
Be suspicious of unusually high pay for simple tasks

classificationMatrix.description: Signal → fraud type mapping for classification engine

bank-immediate-psr.name: Your Bank (PSR)
bank-immediate-psr.instruction: Call number on back of card, reference APP Fraud PSR reimbursement

platform-report.name: Report to Platform
platform-report.instruction: Use built-in reporting tool on the platform where fraud occurred

aeoPatterns.description: Answer-first patterns for featured snippet targeting

queryTemplates.query: what to do if scammed online UK
queryTemplates.answerStart: If you have been scammed online: 1) Stop all payments 2) Contact your bank immediately 3) Report to Action Fraud at actionfraud.police.uk

queryTemplates.query: how to report investment fraud UK
queryTemplates.answerStart: Report investment fraud to Action Fraud (actionfraud.police.uk) and the FCA (fca.org.uk/scamsmart). Contact your bank immediately if money was transferred.

queryTemplates.query: can I get my money back from a scam
queryTemplates.answerStart: You may be able to recover money through: bank chargeback (card payments), APP fraud reimbursement (bank transfers — PSR rules 2024), or the Financial Ombudsman Service.
